questions.description: "The decision of the House of Lords in Salomon v A Salomon & Co Ltd [1897] AC 22 remains as relevant in Ireland today…
questions.text: "The decision of the House of Lords in Salomon v A Salomon & Co Ltd [1897] AC 22 remains as relevant in Ireland today as it did when it was first decided." You are required to discuss this statement, giving reasons as to whether you agree or disagree with it. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Richard and Sarah are directors and 50:50 shareholders in Real World Apps… Company, Shareholder.
questions.text: Richard and Sarah are directors and 50:50 shareholders in Real World Apps Limited (the "Company") which develops and sells games software for use on smart phones, The Company was formed by both of them after leaving college. The Company has been doing well and generated revenue of over €15 million last year, mostly from sales of the Company's most successful app, Mad Monkeys. Richard thinks there is a better market in war games but he has never raised his desire to develop violent game apps with Sarah, whom he knows of old to be against the proliferation of violence. While both Sarah and Richard have been working on new versions of Mad Monkeys, Richard has secretly been working on a new app called Slasher Killer Combat which as the name suggests is a graphically violent game. While he worked on this in his own time at weekends, he did spend Company time working on the game. He used the Company's software to develop the game and also used the Company's contacts to sell the game through an app website. Sales of the game are currently €50,000 per week in royalties and fees. Richard set up his own company, Richard's Apps Ltd, and it is this company that is receiving the royalties. Richard never told Sarah about his personal venture and Sarah only found out about it though trade contacts. Sarah approaches you for advice. You are required to advise Sarah on the following: (a) whether you believe there have been any breaches of the duties owed to the Company by its director, Richard, under Part 5 of the Companies Act 2014; and (15 marks) (b) the remedies that are available to the Company arising from any breach of duties. (5 marks) In your answer you must distinguish between (a) and (b) and you must cite all relevant case and statute law and attempt to apply these to the facts provided above.

questions.description: David, Deborah and Cindy are equal shareholders, holding 50 shares each in… Company, Shareholder.
questions.text: David, Deborah and Cindy are equal shareholders, holding 50 shares each in Foxtrot Tango Limited (the "Company"), established by their late mother Madge. They received their shares from Madge under her will. The Company is in the business of retailing carpet and its only two directors are David and Deborah who also work in the Company. Cindy left the country a number of years ago and lives with her husband in San Francisco and over the years has not had any involvement in the Company. Once a year she contacts her brother and sister to ask how the Company is doing and usually, following that call, David and Deborah declare a dividend which the Company pays to Cindy, averaging over €50,000 per annum for the last three years. Last Christmas when Cindy came home to Ireland she tentatively enquired from David and Deborah whether they would be interested in buying her shares in the Company. They said they would have to think about it because it was hard to know what the shares were worth. David and Deborah come to you for advice when Cindy contacted them again and announced that she had sold her shares in the Company to Joe, a local businessman in the same town as where the Company has its premises. A couple of days later David and Deborah were approached by Joe who gave them a stock transfer form signed by Cindy transferring her 50 shares to him, and he asked them to register him as a member. David and Deborah are unhappy to share the family business with an outsider and ask you for your advice. On examination of the Company's constitution you see that the optional provision set out in section 95(1)(a) of the Companies act 2014 applies and that the directors: "...may in their absolute discretion and without assigning any reason for doing so, decline to register any transfer of any share". You are required to advise David and Deborah on their rights and obligations in relation to the transfer of shares to Joe. In your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: William's World of Colours Limited (the "Company") is a company engaged in… Company, Director.
questions.text: William's World of Colours Limited (the "Company") is a company engaged in the business of manufacturing and distributing paint to the Irish construction industry. For the past number of years, business has been booming. Two of the Company's customers have had lucrative contracts for the painting of new housing estates. The Company has three directors --- William, Peggy and Eugene --- and is a single-member private limited company, all shares being owned by William. William is the son of the Company's founder and is the managing director. Peggy, his daughter, is the Sales Director and Eugene is her husband. Eugene is a non-executive director who works as a local self-employed solicitor. His involvement with the Company is very limited and he rarely if ever attends meetings of the directors. In recent times the Company's fortunes have faltered. Both of its major customers have gone into liquidation, owing the Company several hundred thousand euro. Both customers were always slow payers, but the Company received nothing from them for months although it continued to supply them with paint. William realised things were bad when the Company's bank manager telephoned him to say that the Company had exceeded its overdraft. William persuaded the bank to extend the overdraft to pay wages and give him time to collect some outstanding debts. The Company chugged along for another month but then William realised that the Company was insolvent and without any hope of an immediate improvement. At that point William placed the Company into creditors' voluntary liquidation. The Company's liquidator discovered that the Company's record keeping was sporadic, and that management accounts had not been prepared with the frequency which would have alerted the Company's directors to the fact its two main customers has not been discharging what was owed by them for some time. You are required to advise on the likelihood that each of the three directors will be made the subject of a restriction order pursuant to section 819 of the Companies Act 2014. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Bob, Patrick, and Patty are directors of B-P-P Limited (the "Company")… Company, Shareholder.
questions.text: Bob, Patrick, and Patty are directors of B-P-P Limited (the "Company") which runs an estate agency. They are also shareholders in the Company and each hold 25 of the 100 issued shares, the other 25 shares being held by John. John is a solicitor and has never been a director of the company, being content to be just a shareholder. The premises from which the Company operates is owned by John and leased to the Company at a very favourable rent for the Company. Bob, Patrick and Patty have traditionally recommended that purchasers and vendors of properties that go through the agency use John's solicitors practice for their conveyancing and this has been a very lucrative source of revenue to John. John would in turn recommend to his clients that they use the Company as their estate agent. In recent times, however, John's recommendations have been few and far between and Bob, Patrick and Patty have been recommending other firms of solicitors to clients of the Company as it seems to them that these other solicitors are in a better position than John to make cross-referrals back to the Company. On average, the Company would have made about 30 referrals to John every month; however, for the last 6 months, this has fallen steadily and last month, there were only 5 referrals to him. John was unsure as to why referrals were falling off and had blamed it on the economic down turn until one of his friends in another firm of solicitors (who did not know John was a shareholder in the Company) mentioned that he and the Company were cross-referring clients very successfully. John was very annoyed and confronted Bob, Patrick and Patty, claiming that in ceasing to maintain his level of referrals they were acting in disregard of his interests as a shareholder and pointing to the below market rent he charged the Company for its premises. John also objected to not being told about their decision to make referrals elsewhere. Their response was that they were acting in the interests of the Company and had never asked for a lower rent and that he was welcome to seek to review the rent if he thought it was not at market value. They also indicated that if this was John's attitude, they would not make any referrals to him, a statement which provoked John to claim that they were acting oppressively. You are required to advise on the following matters: (a) whether John has a remedy under section 212 of the Companies Act 2014; and (15 marks) (b) whether your answer would be different if the Company's constitution contained a provision that the Company wouId support shareholders' businesses by referring clients to them. (5 marks) Your answer must address each of the questions at (a) and (b) and you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: "It is now well recognised that a company's members can give their sanction to a course of action in either of three…
questions.text: "It is now well recognised that a company's members can give their sanction to a course of action in either of three ways: first, by a resolution passed at a general meeting of the company, duly convened and held; secondly, by unanimous written resolution and thirdly, by an estoppel, created through their unanimous informal agreement." You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: "In addition to consolidating the law, the Companies Act 2014 has introduced significant reforms to Irish company law".
questions.text: "In addition to consolidating the law, the Companies Act 2014 has introduced significant reforms to Irish company law". You are required to identify and discuss the implications of what you view to be the five main reforms introduced by the Companies Act 2014. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law in support of your views.

questions.description: You are required to: (a) Describe the nature and characteristics of a…
questions.text: You are required to: (a) Describe the nature and characteristics of a floating charge; and (5 marks) (b) Identify the circumstances in which a floating charge will crystallise. (15 marks) (c) In your answer you must distinguish (a) and (b) and you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: "While company directors have a fiduciary duty to act in the interests of their company, they also have duties to have…
questions.text: "While company directors have a fiduciary duty to act in the interests of their company, they also have duties to have regard to the interests of other persons". You are required to discuss and explain this statement. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Harry is an insolvency practitioner and was recently appointed by Big Bank… Company, Shareholder.
questions.text: Harry is an insolvency practitioner and was recently appointed by Big Bank plc (the "Bank") to be receiver to Totally PC Coffee Limited (the "Company") on foot of a debenture the Company had given to the Bank to secure its borrowings. The Bank is owed over €2m by the Company and the Company's principal asset is a premises in an industrial estate in North County Dublin where it mixes, packages and distributes coffee beans sourced from a fair trade plantation in Brazil. The premises was acquired by the Company for €2.5m in 2007. Harry believes that the premises is currently worth in the region of €1.5m and has a firm offer from an interested party for that amount. There is another interested party who has said to Harry that he might be prepared to offer €1.9m in another three months when he can raise the cash. Harry is also receiving telephone calls on a daily basis from Richard, the managing director and sole shareholder in the Company, asking Harry to make sure he gets the best price for the property. Harry is aware that Richard gave a personal guarantee to the Bank, and is aware that if there is a shortfall between the price received for the property and the amount owing to the Bank, he as guarantor will be liable in that amount. You are required to advise Harry on his duties as a receiver, having particular regard to the duties owed to the Company, and also whether he owes any separate duties to a guarantor. In your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: "The doctrine of ostensible authority, the Rule in Turquand's Case and section 40 of the Companies Act 2014 are…
questions.text: "The doctrine of ostensible authority, the Rule in Turquand's Case and section 40 of the Companies Act 2014 are effective in ensuring the enforceability of a contract that has been entered into by a company, acting by its directors" You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: You are approached for legal advice by Gilbert, a court appointed… Company, Shareholder.
questions.text: You are approached for legal advice by Gilbert, a court appointed liquidator who was appointed to the Plot Gets Thicker Limited (the "Company"), which had been a subcontractor in the film production business. Gilbert was appointed liquidator on foot of a petition brought by the Revenue Commissioners to have the company wound up for failure to pay its debts. It appears that the Company is hopelessly insolvent, having debts in excess of €15m of which some €5m is owed to the Revenue Commissioners. Following his appointment, Gilbert began to investigate the Company's affairs and the behaviour of the Company's sole-shareholder and managing director, Reynard Fox ("Mr Fox"). Gilbert tells you: (a) The Company's books and records are in a sorry state. Record keeping has been at best sporadic, and Gilbert is finding it difficult to piece together the Company's financial history. It appears the film extras were often paid in cash and kept off the books of the Company as there are no records of people having being paid. However, other documents suggest that the Company had supplied hundreds of extras in a recent film in which it had been involved. Basic invoice keeping also appears to have been ignored and there is little or no financial documentation to support the claims which a number of creditors have lodged against the company for both goods and services allegedly supplied. (b) The Company has also incurred massive debts with other film companies. Its modus operandi seems to have been to agree to secure the acting services of "big-name" stars for up-coming films in return for the production companies paying the Company a significant fee of between €500,000 and €1m. Gilbert has discovered that the Company was a party to no fewer than 6 such agreements with 6 different production companies and while it received some €5m from this business, it never once managed to procure the star for the film. The 6 creditors in question have complained to Gilbert that the Company and Mr Fox traded fraudulently or, at best, recklessly in carrying on this business. Gilbert has found no evidence that the Company even sought the services of the stars in question, after taking the creditors' money. You are required to advise Gilbert on the grounds for applying to the High Court to have Mr Fox made personally liable for some or all of the Company's debts and the law in relation to the facts set out in (a) and (b). In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: "When proceedings are brought against the director of an insolvent company under Section 819 of the Companies Act 2014,…
questions.text: "When proceedings are brought against the director of an insolvent company under Section 819 of the Companies Act 2014, case law indicates that unless the director can show that he or she acted honestly and responsibly in relation to the conduct of the affairs of the company, the Court will make a restriction order". You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: You are approached for advice by Denis, the financial director of a medium… Company, Shareholder.
questions.text: You are approached for advice by Denis, the financial director of a medium sized company called Festive Tidings Limited (the "Company") which distributes Christmas greeting cards. The company has three directors (of which he is one) and is a wholly-owned subsidiary of an English company called Festive Greetings (UK) Limited. The English company holds the subsidiary's only issued share which has a nominal value of €1. Denis tells you that he and his fellow directors are coming under increasing pressure to sanction a distribution to the holding company --- which is in some financial difficulties in the sum of €1m. Denis is concerned about this because while the Company is solvent and would be solvent after the payment of the € 1m to the holding company, the Company has only €500,000 in available distributable profits. Notwithstanding this, Denis is concerned that the holding company may pass a resolution directing the directors of the Company to pay the €1m sought. The Company's constitution expressly adopts the "optional provisions" contained in the Companies Act 2014 so Chapter 7 of Part 3 of the Companies Act 2014 applies. You are required to advise Denis and his fellow directors on the law relating to distributions and to the rights, duties and liabilities of directors and shareholders in relation to distributions. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law and attempt to apply these to the facts provided above.

questions.description: "Although section 212 of the Companies Act 2014 provides a powerful remedy for oppressed shareholders, the exceptions…
questions.text: "Although section 212 of the Companies Act 2014 provides a powerful remedy for oppressed shareholders, the exceptions to the Rule in Foss v Harbottle and the derivative action continue to be important mechanisms for holding errant directors to account". You are required to discuss this statement. In your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: The liquidator of Timber Products Limited (the "Company") contacts you for… Company, Director.
questions.text: The liquidator of Timber Products Limited (the "Company") contacts you for advice. The liquidator tells you that he has just been appointed to the Company by the High Court on the petition of the Revenue Commissioners and needs your help in unravelling the Company's affairs and determining the order of priorities. Specifically, you are advised that: (a) A company called Raw Materials Limited has a retention of title clause in its contract for the sale of three batches of timber it supplied to the Company. The Liquidator advises you that there are only two batches untouched as the third was made into furniture. The clause in question provided "The property in the timber shall not transfer to the Company until all sums owing to the Vendor have been paid and until then any goods made from the timber shall be the property of the Vendor'. The Liquidator wants to know whether he must return the timber and the furniture made therefrom. (b) Another supplier claims to have a fixed charge over one of the Company's bank accounts. On further enquiry the Liquidator was shown a copy of a deed of fixed charge over the Company's book debts which required them to be paid into a designated bank account. As far as the Liquidator can see the account was operated by the Company whose directors lodged and withdrew monies therefrom at will. The liquidator wants to know whether this charge gives the supplier priority over the Revenue Commissioners. You are required to advise the Liquidator in respect of each of the foregoing matters. Your answer should clearly distinguish the two questions asked in (a) and (b) and you must cite all relevant case and statute law.
